content.text: Non-metals are elements that lack metallic properties. Tini haru usually brittle, poor conductors of heat and electricity huncha, and gain electrons in reactions to form negative ions (anions).

① Usually gases or solids at room temperature (exception: Bromine \( Br_2 \) is a liquid).
② Brittle when in solid state — bend garda toot-cha.
③ Dull in appearance (non-lustrous).
④ Low density and low melting/boiling points (generally).

① Tend to gain electrons → form anions (negative ions).
② React with metals → form ionic compounds.
③ React with other non-metals → form covalent compounds.

• Generally poor conductors of heat and electricity.
• Exception: Graphite (allotrope of carbon) conducts electricity due to delocalized π electrons.

• Gases: \( O_2,\ N_2,\ Cl_2,\ F_2,\ H_2 \), Noble gases
• Solids: S, P, \( I_2 \), C, Si
• Liquid (only one): \( Br_2 \)

• Found on the right-hand side of the periodic table.
• Groups 14–18 (except H — top of Group 1, but a non-metal).
• Separated from metals by the 'metalloid staircase' (B, Si, Ge, As, Sb, Te).

• Hydrogen (H), Carbon (C), Nitrogen (N), Oxygen (O), Sulphur (S), Phosphorus (P)
• Halogens: F, Cl, Br, I, At
• Noble Gases: He, Ne, Ar, Kr, Xe

• Across a period (left → right): Reactivity ↑ (electronegativity increases).
• Down a group: Reactivity ↓ (atomic size increases → weaker electron attraction).

• Non-metals have high electronegativity → strong tendency to attract electrons.
• Fluorine (F) is the most electronegative element in the entire periodic table.

• High ionization energy → difficult to remove electrons from non-metals.
• This is why non-metals gain electrons rather than lose them.

• \( O_2 \) → respiration, combustion, steel production
• \( N_2 \) → fertilizers (via \( NH_3 \) synthesis), food packaging
• S → matches, gunpowder, vulcanization of rubber
• P → fertilizers, explosives, safety matches
• Halogens: \( Cl_2 \) → water disinfection; \( I_2 \) → antiseptics, medicine

content.label: 🎓 Key Points to Remember:
content.text:
✓ Non-metals → right side of periodic table (Groups 14–18 + H).
✓ Brittle, non-lustrous, low conductors (except graphite).
✓ Gain electrons → form anions; high electronegativity & ionisation energy.
✓ React with metals → ionic bonds; react with non-metals → covalent bonds.
✓ Only liquid non-metal at RT = Br₂; only conducting non-metal = graphite.
✓ Most electronegative element = Fluorine (F).
